title: Comparative assessment of germanium-based spin-qubit modalities: donor, acceptor, gate-defined hole, and gate-defined electron platforms
authors: D. -M. Mei, K. -M. Dong, S. A. Panamaldeniya, A. Prem, S. Chhetri, N. Budhathoki, S. Bhattarai
abstract: High-purity germanium (Ge) has re-emerged as a versatile semiconductor platform for spin-based quantum information processing because it combines mature materials processing, access to spin-free isotopes, high mobilities, small effective masses, and strong but engineerable spin--orbit coupling. However, ``Ge qubits'' are not a single technology. Donor spin qubits, acceptor spin qubits, gate-defined hole spin qubits, and gate-defined electron spin qubits exploit different parts of the Ge band structure and therefore make distinct trade-offs among coherence, controllability, fabrication complexity, and scalability. Here we compare these four Ge-based spin-qubit modalities on a common physical and architectural footing. We review the shared Ge materials physics, including isotopic purification, the multivalley \(L\)-point conduction band, the spin-\(3/2\) valence band, heavy-hole/light-hole mixing, strain, interfaces, disorder, and phonons. We also introduce a common framework for estimating phononic-crystal-modified \(T_1\) using a calibrated reference relaxation rate, a geometry-dependent strain-density-of-states suppression factor, and parasitic relaxation channels. The comparison shows that gate-defined Ge hole-spin qubits currently offer the strongest combination of all-electrical control, demonstrated multiqubit operation, and scalability. Donor, acceptor, and gate-defined electron qubits remain important complementary directions for memory, hybrid, and exploratory architectures. Overall, Ge supports a diverse qubit ecosystem, with gate-defined hole-spin qubits presently providing the clearest path toward scalable Ge-based quantum processors.
summary: This paper provides a comprehensive comparison of four different ways to implement spin qubits using germanium. It concludes that while gate-defined hole qubits are currently the best candidate for scalable quantum computing, the different Ge-based technologies can work together in a specialized ecosystem for memory and hybrid processing.
main_problem: The lack of a unified comparative framework to evaluate the trade-offs between different germanium-based spin-qubit modalities (donor, acceptor, gate-defined hole, and gate-defined electron) regarding coherence, controllability, and scalability.
main_result: The study identifies gate-defined hole-spin qubits as the most mature and promising path for scalable processors, while proposing a layered ecosystem where donors and acceptors serve specialized roles in memory and hybrid architectures.
method: A comparative technical assessment using a literature-based review, scaling arguments, and a new theoretical framework for estimating phononic-crystal-modified T1 relaxation.
model_or_system: High-purity germanium (Ge) platforms including donor-bound electrons, acceptor-bound holes, and gate-defined electron and hole quantum dots in Ge/SiGe heterostructures.
key_observables: Spin-lattice relaxation rate (T1), coherence time (T2), g-tensor anisotropy, valley splitting, and gate fidelity.
important_parameters: Spin-orbit coupling strength, effective masses (longitudinal/transverse), isotopic purity (5N to 13N), and impurity-induced strain scales.
main_assumptions: The comparison assumes that the developmental trajectory of each modality can be extrapolated from current experimental milestones and materials physics.
figures_summary: Figure 1 visualizes the relationship between average linear strain and residual impurity density in germanium.
paper_structure: The paper begins with a review of Ge materials physics, differentiates the four qubit modalities based on band structure (L-point vs. Gamma-point), introduces a framework for relaxation estimation, and concludes with a strategic roadmap for a layered quantum ecosystem.
why_interesting: It provides a high-level strategic analysis of semiconductor-based quantum architectures, which is relevant for understanding the physical limits of scalable hardware and the engineering of spin-photon/spin-phonon interfaces.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Estimated average linear strain in Ge as a function of residual impurity density for B, Al, Ga, P, As, and Sb, calculated from |¯εi| ≈|ηi|ni/N0. The strain decreases linearly with impurity density, demonstrating how improved purity directly suppresses the background strain field. B, P, and Sb generate the largest strain because their size mismatch relative to Ge is largest. Only five distinct curves are visible because the Al and As curves overlap exactly: they have equal absolute mismatch parameters and therefore give the same |¯εi|. The dashed vertical lines mark representative impurity levels for 13N, 9N, and 5N Ge. The plot is intended as an order-of-magnitude comparative...

Figure 2: Conceptual schematic of a Ge donor-spin platform integrated with a phononic crystal. (a) Top-view architecture showing P donors embedded in a Ge substrate, controlled by local barrier gates and a nearby charge sensor, with microwave excitation used for spin manipulation. The patterned phononic crystal surrounding a central defect region is intended to create a phononic bandgap and to localize selected acoustic modes near the donor site. (b) Cross-sectional schematic illustrating the vertical device structure and the role of the phononic crystal cavity in shaping the local acoustic environment. In this architecture, phononic bandgap engineering is intended to suppress unwanted...

Figure 3: Conceptual schematic of a Ge acceptor-spin platform integrated with a phononic crystal. (a) Device-level architecture showing a single acceptor, illustrated here as a B acceptor, embedded in Ge and controlled by local control and readout gates, with the surrounding phononic crystal engineered to create a bandgap and a localized defect cavity near the qubit. This architecture is intended to combine electrical tuning of the acceptor-bound hole with control of the local acoustic environment. (b) Effective-level diagram illustrating the interface- and field-split acceptor mani- fold, where the lower-energy Kramers doublets can be tuned by electric field and coupled through strain- and...

Figure 4: Conceptual schematic of a gate-defined Ge hole-spin qubit platform integrated with a phononic crystal cavity. (a) Top-view architecture showing a strained Ge quantum well formed in a Ge/SiGe heterostructure, where local electrostatic gates define a hole-spin qubit and a nearby charge sensor enables electrical readout. A pulsed microwave signal applied to the gate stack drives the qubit electrically, while the patterned phononic crystal surrounding a central defect cavity is intended to create a phononic bandgap and localize selected acoustic modes near the qubit. (b) Cross-sectional schematic illustrating the vertical device structure and the role of the phononic cavity in...

Figure 5: Conceptual schematic of a gate-defined Ge electron-spin qubit platform integrated with a phononic crystal cavity. (a) Top-view architecture showing a strain-engineered Ge/SiGe het- erostructure in which electrostatic gates define a lateral electron-spin qubit and a nearby charge sensor provides electrical readout. The control-gate region is used to tune the confinement poten- tial and manipulate the electron state, while the patterned phononic crystal surrounding a central defect cavity is intended to create a phononic bandgap and localize selected acoustic modes near the qubit.
